what:
Microsoft Graph Language Pack for OpenFn. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to Microsoft Graph.

It exposes 6 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. Vendor homepage: https://developer.microsoft.com/graph.
connect:
Microsoft supports OAuth 2.0 for API access.

The credential schema asks for apiVersion and access_token (required). Point the credential at the base URL that matches your environment: https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0, https://graph.microsoft.com/beta. Auth reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/auth/.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in Microsoft Graph.

5 triggers are catalogued (4 webhook, 1 polling), including New Outlook Email, New Excel Row, New SharePoint File, New Teams Message, and more. See the Triggers tab for the full list. Microsoft can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/graph/webhooks), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (webhook-driven where supported, polling otherwise). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Microsoft. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Microsoft jobs at volume:

- Prefer Microsoft webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Paginate list and search calls explicitly; never assume a single response is the full result set.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: OData-based; CSDL metadata also available at https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/$metadata.
best:
- Store Microsoft credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-msgraph to v0.8.13 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
